skills.skill: Change: Introducing Calculus: Can Change Occur at an Instant?
skills.prompt: This topic introduces the foundational question of calculus for AP Calculus BC students: can change occur at an instant? (CED: CHA-1.A, Unit 1). Students explore how average rates of change over shrinking intervals approach an instantaneous rate of change via limits. Key concepts include the difference quotient [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) as average rate of change, the transition from secant lines to tangent lines, and real-world contexts like instantaneous velocity. In BC, this concept extends into parametric and vector-valued contexts where instantaneous rates of change apply to each component. Students should connect this to the limit definition of the derivative and understand that calculus formalizes the idea of instantaneous change. AP exam note: BC students should be comfortable interpreting rates of change in multidimensional motion contexts as well as single-variable contexts. Vocabulary: average rate of change, instantaneous rate of change, secant line, difference quotient, limit, tangent.

skills.skill: Limits: Defining Limits and Using Limit Notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers defining limits and using limit notation for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.A, LIM-1.B, Unit 1). The limit of f(x) as x approaches c, written lim[x->c] f(x) = L, means f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L for x sufficiently close to c (but x != c). Key concepts: one-sided limits (left-hand lim[x->c-] and right-hand lim[x->c+]); a two-sided limit exists if and only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal; the limit may exist even when f(c) is undefined or differs from L. BC students use limits extensively in series convergence, improper integrals, and parametric/polar contexts. Students should read limits from graphs, tables, and algebraic expressions. AP exam note: limit notation and concepts are foundational to all BC topics including sequences, series, and convergence tests. Vocabulary: limit, left-hand limit, right-hand limit, two-sided limit, notation, approaches, epsilon-delta.

skills.skill: Change: Estimating Limit Values from Graphs
skills.prompt: This topic covers estimating limit values from graphs for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.C, Unit 1). Students read graphs to determine one-sided and two-sided limits by observing the y-value a function approaches near a given x-value. Key concepts: distinguishing f(c) from lim[x->c] f(x); identifying when limits do not exist due to differing one-sided behaviors; recognizing unbounded behavior indicating infinite limits; reading open and closed circles correctly on graphs of piecewise functions. BC students apply graphical limit estimation to more complex functions, including those arising from parametric or polar representations. Students should be comfortable with graphs showing jumps, holes, and vertical asymptotes. AP exam note: graphical limit questions appear in multiple-choice sections and as parts of free-response problems involving accumulation functions and series. Vocabulary: graphical limit, open circle, closed circle, piecewise, jump discontinuity, unbounded.

skills.skill: Limits: Estimating Limit Values from Tables
skills.prompt: This topic covers estimating limits from numerical tables for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.C, Unit 1). Students examine function values at inputs approaching the target from both sides to estimate limits. Key concepts: constructing tables with x-values like c-0.1, c-0.01, c-0.001 and c+0.1, c+0.01, c+0.001; observing trends in f(x) to estimate the limit; recognizing when numerical evidence suggests the limit does not exist (oscillation, divergent values). Tables provide estimates, not proofs. BC students apply this skill in broader contexts including convergence of sequences and estimating series sums. Students should understand limitations of numerical methods and verify with analytical techniques when possible. AP exam note: tabular data appears frequently in BC free-response problems, often requiring estimation of derivatives and integrals alongside limits. Vocabulary: numerical estimate, approaching, trend, table of values, left-hand, right-hand, convergence.

skills.skill: Limits: Determining Limits Using Algebraic Properties of Limits
skills.prompt: This topic covers algebraic limit properties for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.D, Unit 1). If lim[x->c] f(x) and lim[x->c] g(x) both exist, limits preserve arithmetic: limit of a sum = sum of limits, limit of a product = product of limits, limit of a quotient = quotient of limits (denominator != 0), and limit of [f(x)]^n = [lim f(x)]^n. Direct substitution works for polynomials and rational functions where the denominator is nonzero. These properties extend to compositions under appropriate continuity conditions. BC students apply these properties in more complex settings including sequences (limit laws for sequences), power series, and convergence tests. Mastery of limit properties enables efficient computation throughout the course. AP exam note: these properties are used implicitly in nearly every convergence test and derivative/integral computation. Vocabulary: limit laws, sum rule, product rule, quotient rule, direct substitution, composition.

skills.skill: Limits: Determining Limits Using Algebraic Manipulation
skills.prompt: This topic covers algebraic manipulation for evaluating limits in AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.E, Unit 1). When direct substitution yields 0/0, students manipulate the expression: factoring and canceling, conjugate multiplication for radicals, simplifying complex fractions, using trigonometric identities, and expanding expressions. Foundational limits: lim[x->0] sin(x)/x = 1 and lim[x->0] (1-cos(x))/x = 0. BC students encounter these techniques in more sophisticated contexts including evaluating limits that arise in series convergence tests and improper integral evaluation. Students should recognize the indeterminate form and select the appropriate algebraic technique to resolve it. AP exam note: algebraic manipulation of limits is tested throughout the BC exam, including in the context of L'Hopital's Rule (which provides an alternative when available). Vocabulary: indeterminate form, factoring, conjugate, rationalize, simplify, trigonometric identity, 0/0.

skills.skill: Limits: Selecting Procedures for Determining Limits
skills.prompt: This topic covers selecting limit evaluation procedures for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.D, LIM-1.E, Unit 1). Students strategically choose among: direct substitution, factoring, conjugate multiplication, simplification, trigonometric identities, or the Squeeze Theorem. Decision process: (1) try direct substitution; (2) if finite, that is the limit; (3) if nonzero/zero, limit is infinite or DNE; (4) if 0/0, apply algebraic manipulation or L'Hopital's Rule. BC students must also consider whether series-related limit techniques (Ratio Test, Root Test) are appropriate. This topic synthesizes all limit techniques and emphasizes efficient problem-solving. AP exam note: the ability to quickly identify the right approach is critical for time management on the BC exam, which has additional series and parametric content. Vocabulary: procedure selection, direct substitution, indeterminate form, factoring, conjugate, L'Hopital's Rule, efficiency.

skills.skill: Limits: Determining Limits Using the Squeeze Theorem
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Squeeze Theorem for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.E, Unit 1). If g(x) <= f(x) <= h(x) near c, and lim[x->c] g(x) = lim[x->c] h(x) = L, then lim[x->c] f(x) = L. This is essential for limits involving oscillating functions, such as lim[x->0] x^2 sin(1/x) = 0. Key concepts: identifying appropriate bounding functions, verifying the inequality near the point, and confirming matching limits of the bounds. BC students also apply squeeze-type reasoning in sequence and series contexts. The Squeeze Theorem for sequences states: if a_n <= b_n <= c_n and lim a_n = lim c_n = L, then lim b_n = L. AP exam note: the Squeeze Theorem appears occasionally and tests the student's ability to identify bounds and justify the argument rigorously. Vocabulary: Squeeze Theorem, Sandwich Theorem, bounding functions, inequality, oscillating function, sequences.

skills.skill: Limits: Connecting Multiple Representations of Limits
skills.prompt: This topic covers connecting graphical, numerical, and analytical limit representations for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-1.A, LIM-1.B, LIM-1.C, Unit 1). Students should move fluidly between graph-based estimation, table-based estimation, and algebraic computation of limits. Each representation provides complementary information: graphs show overall behavior, tables give numerical evidence, and algebra provides exact values. Students should verify consistency across representations and understand each method's limitations. BC students apply multi-representational reasoning throughout the course, particularly when analyzing series convergence (graphing partial sums, computing terms numerically, proving convergence analytically). AP exam note: free-response questions frequently present mixed representations. Students should explain how evidence from one representation supports conclusions drawn from another. Vocabulary: multiple representations, graphical, numerical, analytical, consistency, verification, complementary.

This topic covers types of discontinuities for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-2.A, Unit 1). Students classify discontinuities as: (1) Removable (hole): limit exists but does not equal f(c); (2) Jump: left and right limits exist but differ; (3) Infinite: function grows without bound, producing a vertical asymptote. Key concepts: identifying each type from graphs and formulas, understanding that removable discontinuities arise from common factors in rational functions, and recognizing piecewise functions may have jump discontinuities. BC students encounter discontinuities in more complex contexts including parametric curves (where t-values may produce discontinuities in dy/dx) and when analyzing convergence of series at interval endpoints. AP exam note: classification of discontinuities is foundational knowledge expected throughout the BC exam. Vocabulary: removable discontinuity, jump discontinuity, infinite discontinuity, hole, vertical asymptote, piecewise, classification.

skills.skill: Analysis of Functions: Defining Continuity at a Point
skills.prompt: This topic covers the formal definition of continuity at a point for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-2.A, Unit 1). A function f is continuous at x = c if: (1) f(c) is defined, (2) lim[x->c] f(x) exists, and (3) lim[x->c] f(x) = f(c). If any condition fails, f is discontinuous at c. Polynomials, exponentials, and trig functions are continuous on their domains. Rational functions are continuous where denominators are nonzero. BC students apply continuity in the hypotheses of major theorems (IVT, MVT, EVT, FTC) and in analyzing convergence of power series at endpoints. Students should determine parameter values that ensure continuity of piecewise functions. AP exam note: the three-part definition is required for justifying continuity in free-response answers throughout the BC course. Vocabulary: continuous at a point, three conditions, defined, limit exists, limit equals value, piecewise, theorem hypothesis.

skills.skill: Limits: Confirming Continuity over an Interval
skills.prompt: This topic covers confirming continuity over an interval for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-2.B, Unit 1). A function is continuous on an open interval if continuous at every point; on [a,b] it additionally requires right-continuity at a and left-continuity at b. Common continuous functions: polynomials (everywhere), rational functions (domain), trig, exponential, logarithmic (their domains). Compositions and combinations of continuous functions are continuous where defined. BC students verify interval continuity as a prerequisite for applying the IVT, MVT, EVT, and FTC. For power series, the sum function is continuous inside the interval of convergence. AP exam note: students must explicitly verify continuity when applying theorems that require it, especially on free-response questions. Vocabulary: continuous on an interval, open interval, closed interval, right-continuous, left-continuous, domain, theorem prerequisite.

This topic covers removing discontinuities for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-2.C, Unit 1). A removable discontinuity at x = c can be removed by defining f(c) = lim[x->c] f(x). For rational functions, removable discontinuities arise from common factors that cancel. Key steps: factor numerator and denominator, cancel common factors, evaluate the simplified expression at c to find the y-coordinate of the hole. Only removable discontinuities can be fixed; jump and infinite discontinuities cannot. BC students encounter removable discontinuities when analyzing rational functions in partial fraction decomposition and when examining series-related functions. AP exam note: identifying holes in rational functions and making piecewise functions continuous are common question types throughout the BC exam. Vocabulary: removable discontinuity, hole, redefine, continuous extension, common factor, piecewise continuity.

skills.skill: Limits: Connecting Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes
skills.prompt: This topic covers infinite limits and vertical asymptotes for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-2.D, Unit 1). When lim[x->c] f(x) = +/- infinity, f has a vertical asymptote at x = c. The limit does not exist as a finite number. For rational functions p(x)/q(x), vertical asymptotes occur where q(c) = 0 but p(c) != 0. Sign analysis near the asymptote determines the direction (+infinity or -infinity from each side). Logarithmic functions have vertical asymptotes at the boundary of their domain. BC students encounter vertical asymptotes in polar curves, improper integrals, and when analyzing convergence of series (e.g., integral test with functions having vertical asymptotes). AP exam note: students must clearly state that infinite limits mean the limit DNE as a finite value while describing the unbounded behavior. Vocabulary: infinite limit, vertical asymptote, unbounded, sign analysis, one-sided, domain boundary.

skills.skill: Limits: Connecting Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes
skills.prompt: This topic covers limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-2.D, Unit 1). If lim[x->infinity] f(x) = L, then y = L is a horizontal asymptote. For rational functions: if deg(num) < deg(den), HA is y = 0; if degrees are equal, HA is y = (leading coefficients ratio); if deg(num) > deg(den), no HA. Technique: divide by highest power of x in denominator. End behavior of exponentials (e^x dominates polynomials) and logarithms (dominated by polynomials) is important. BC students use limits at infinity extensively in series (nth term test, ratio test, limit comparison test) and when evaluating improper integrals. Growth rate hierarchy: logarithmic < polynomial < exponential < factorial. AP exam note: comparing growth rates is essential for convergence tests in the BC series unit. Vocabulary: limit at infinity, horizontal asymptote, end behavior, dominant term, growth rate hierarchy, leading coefficient.

skills.skill: Limits: Working with the Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Intermediate Value Theorem for AP Calculus BC (CED: FUN-1.A, Unit 1). The IVT states: if f is continuous on [a,b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists c in (a,b) with f(c) = N. Key concepts: the IVT guarantees existence only (not location); continuity is the required hypothesis; commonly used to prove existence of roots (if f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, f has a zero in (a,b)). BC students apply the IVT in the same way as AB but may encounter it in more complex contexts involving parametric functions or series-defined functions. Students must explicitly verify continuity and that N lies between f(a) and f(b) when invoking the theorem. AP exam note: IVT justification requires three components: state continuity, identify the interval, and confirm the intermediate value condition. Vocabulary: Intermediate Value Theorem, continuous, existence, root, zero, closed interval, hypothesis.
